subject: Chemistry
q: Consider the figure provided. \(1 \mathrm{~mol}\) of an ideal gas is kept in a cylinder, fitted with a piston, at the position A, at \(18^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\). If the piston is moved to position \(\mathrm{B}\), keeping the temperature unchanged, then ' \(x\) ' \(L\) atm work is done in this reversible process. \(x=\) ______ \(\mathrm{L}\) atm. (nearest integer) [Given : Absolute temperature \(={ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}+273.15, \mathrm{R}=0.08206 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~mol}{ }^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\) ]
question: Consider the figure provided. \(1 \mathrm{~mol}\) of an ideal gas is kept in a cylinder, fitted with a piston, at the position A, at \(18^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\). If the piston is moved to position \(\mathrm{B}\), keeping the temperature unchanged, then ' \(x\) ' \(L\) atm work is done in this reversible process. \(x=\) ______ \(\mathrm{L}\) atm. (nearest integer) [Given : Absolute temperature \(={ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}+273.15, \mathrm{R}=0.08206 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~mol}{ }^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\) ]
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=-n R T \ln \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \\ & =-1 \times \cdot 08206 \times 291 \cdot 15 \ln \left(\frac{100}{10}\right) \\ & =-55.0128\end{aligned}$
Work done by system $\approx 55 \mathrm{~atm}$ lit.

The additional information required to determine the average energy for $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond formation would be
question: The standard enthalpy of formation $\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\right)$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ for methane, $\mathrm{CH}_4(\mathrm{~g})$, is $-74.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. latent heat of vapourization of methane
the first four ionization energies of carbon and electron gain enthalpy of hydrogen

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given:

Cgraphite+O2g→CO2g ;ΔrHo=−393.5 kJ mol−1

H2g+12O2g→H2Ol;ΔrHo=-285.8 kJ mol-1

CO2g+2H2Ol→CH4g+2O2g;ΔrHo=+890.3 kJ mol-1

Based on the above thermochemical equations, the value of ΔrHo at 298 K for the reaction

Cgraphite+2H2g→CH4g will be:
question:
Given:

Cgraphite+O2g→CO2g ;ΔrHo=−393.5 kJ mol−1

H2g+12O2g→H2Ol;ΔrHo=-285.8 kJ mol-1

CO2g+2H2Ol→CH4g+2O2g;ΔrHo=+890.3 kJ mol-1

Based on the above thermochemical equations, the value of ΔrHo at 298 K for the reaction

Cgraphite+2H2g→CH4g will be:
solution:
The standard enthalpy of formation is defined as the change in enthalpy when one mole of a substance in the standard state (1 atm of pressure and 298.15 K) is formed from its pure elements under the same conditions.

The enthalpy of combustion of a substance is defined as the heat energy given out when one mole of a substance burns completely in oxygen.

The formation reaction of methane is 

Cgraphite+2H2g→CH4g

The heat of combustion data for C(graphite), H2g and CH4g

∆Hfo=∑∆HCombustionreactants-∑∆HCombustionproducts∆HfoCH4=∆HCoCgraphite+2×∆HCoH2g-∆HCoCH4g

∆HfoCH4=-393.5-2×285.8+890.3
ΔfHo of CH4 (g)=−74.8 kJ/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: The enthalpy of neutralisation of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ with $\mathrm{HCl}$ is $-51.46 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and the enthalpy of neutralisation of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ with $\mathrm{HCl}$ is $-55.90 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The enthalpy of ionisation of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ is
question: The enthalpy of neutralisation of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ with $\mathrm{HCl}$ is $-51.46 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and the enthalpy of neutralisation of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ with $\mathrm{HCl}$ is $-55.90 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The enthalpy of ionisation of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
Strong acid (Complete ionisation)
$$
\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
$$
Weak base $\Delta \mathrm{H}=x \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
\Delta \mathrm{H}=-55.90 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{gathered}
$$
(from neutralisation of strong acid and strong base)
From equation (i), (ii) and (iii)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{H}=-51.46 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
\therefore \quad & x+(-55.90)=-51.46 \\
& x=-51.46+55.90 \\
& =4.44 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Enthalpy of ionisation of
$$
\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}=4.44 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
$$

q: The enthalpy of combustion of propane, graphite and dihydrogen at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> are: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2220</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>393</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>285</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The magnitude enthalpy of formation of propane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>8</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>kJmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: The enthalpy of combustion of propane, graphite and dihydrogen at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> are: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2220</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>393</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>285</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The magnitude enthalpy of formation of propane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>8</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>kJmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The combustion reaction can be written as follows,

C3H8g+5O2→3CO2+4H2O; ∆H=-2220.0 kJ mol-1

Cgraphite+O2g→CO2g ; ∆H=-393.5 kJ mol-1

H2g+12O2g→H2Ol ; ∆H=-285.8 kJ mol-1

Now, the formation reaction of propane is

3Cgraphite+4H2g→C3H8 g∆Hf=3∆HC°graphite+4∆HC°H2g-∆HC°propane∆Hf=3×-393.5+4×-285.8--2220=-103.7 kJ mol-1

subject: Chemistry
q: The standard reaction Gibbs energy for a chemical reaction at an absolute temperature $\mathrm{T}$ is given by $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{BT}$ where A and $B$ are non-zero constants. Which of the following is true about this reaction?
question: The standard reaction Gibbs energy for a chemical reaction at an absolute temperature $\mathrm{T}$ is given by $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{BT}$ where A and $B$ are non-zero constants. Which of the following is true about this reaction?
solution:
$\Delta G^{\circ}=A-B T$

A and B are non-zero constants

$\therefore \Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}=A-B T$

$\therefore$ Reaction will be endothermic if $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}>0 .$

Hence, $\mathrm{A}>0$ and $\mathrm{B}  < 0$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following data:
$\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ (methane, g$)=-\mathrm{X} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
Enthalpy of sublimation of graphite $=\mathrm{Y} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\mathrm{Z} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
The bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is given by :
question:
Consider the following data:
$\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ (methane, g$)=-\mathrm{X} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
Enthalpy of sublimation of graphite $=\mathrm{Y} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\mathrm{Z} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
The bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is given by :
solution:
For methane formation: $\text{C(g)} + 2\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g)$.
Using enthalpy balance: $\Delta_f H° = \text{Bonds broken} - \text{Bonds formed}$.
Breaking: sublimation of C (Y) and 2 H-H bonds (2Z); Forming: 4 C-H bonds.
Energy equation: $-X = Y + 2Z - 4B$ where B is C-H bond enthalpy.
Solving: $4B = X + Y + 2Z$, so $B = \frac{X + Y + 2Z}{4}$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following data.
(i) $2Al(s) + 6HCl(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 3H_2(g) + 1200$ kJ/mol
(ii) $H_2(g) + Cl_2(g) \rightarrow 2HCl(g) + 164$ kJ/mol
(iii) $HCl(g) + aq \rightarrow HCl(aq) + 83$ kJ/mol
(iv) $Al_2Cl_6(s) + aq \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 663$ kJ/mol
The enthalpy of formation of anhydrous solid $Al_2Cl_6$ is :
question:
Consider the following data.
(i) $2Al(s) + 6HCl(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 3H_2(g) + 1200$ kJ/mol
(ii) $H_2(g) + Cl_2(g) \rightarrow 2HCl(g) + 164$ kJ/mol
(iii) $HCl(g) + aq \rightarrow HCl(aq) + 83$ kJ/mol
(iv) $Al_2Cl_6(s) + aq \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 663$ kJ/mol
The enthalpy of formation of anhydrous solid $Al_2Cl_6$ is :
solution:
The given reactions with their enthalpy changes are:
(i) $2Al(s) + 6HCl(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 3H_2(g)$, $\Delta H_1 = -1200$ kJ/mol
(ii) $H_2(g) + Cl_2(g) \rightarrow 2HCl(g)$, $\Delta H_2 = -164$ kJ/mol
(iii) $HCl(g) + aq \rightarrow HCl(aq)$, $\Delta H_3 = -83$ kJ/mol
(iv) $Al_2Cl_6(s) + aq \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq)$, $\Delta H_4 = -663$ kJ/mol

The required reaction for the enthalpy of formation of anhydrous solid $Al_2Cl_6$ is:
$2Al(s) + 3Cl_2(g) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(s)$

This target equation can be obtained by applying the following algebraic operations on the given equations:
Equation (i) $+ 3 \times$ Equation (ii) $+ 6 \times$ Equation (iii) $-$ Equation (iv)

Let us verify by adding the modified equations:
$2Al(s) + 6HCl(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 3H_2(g)$
$3H_2(g) + 3Cl_2(g) \rightarrow 6HCl(g)$
$6HCl(g) + aq \rightarrow 6HCl(aq)$
$Al_2Cl_6(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(s) + aq$

Summing the above reactions and canceling common terms on both sides, we get:
$2Al(s) + 3Cl_2(g) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(s)$

The enthalpy of formation is calculated as:
$\Delta H_f = \Delta H_1 + 3\Delta H_2 + 6\Delta H_3 - \Delta H_4$
$\Delta H_f = -1200 + 3(-164) + 6(-83) - (-663)$
$\Delta H_f = -1200 - 492 - 498 + 663$
$\Delta H_f = -2190 + 663 = -1527$ kJ/mol

Answer: $-1527$ kJ mol$^{-1}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The incorrect statements from the following is:
A. The electrical work that a reaction can perform at constant pressure and temperature is equal to the reaction Gibbs energy.
B. Ecell∘  is dependent on the pressure.
C. dEcell°dT=ΔrS°nF
D. A cell is operating reversibly if the cell potential is exactly balanced by an opposing source of potential difference.
question:
The incorrect statements from the following is:
A. The electrical work that a reaction can perform at constant pressure and temperature is equal to the reaction Gibbs energy.
B. Ecell∘  is dependent on the pressure.
C. dEcell°dT=ΔrS°nF
D. A cell is operating reversibly if the cell potential is exactly balanced by an opposing source of potential difference.
solution:
Ecell∘ is dependent on the equilibrium constant, K, which is not dependent on the pressure of any gas involved in the cell. Relation between Electrochemical Cell Potential and Gibbs free Energy change is given by; Δ G = -nFE where Delta G is Gibbs Free energy change , n is the number of electrons involved in the reaction and F is Faraday and E is the Cell potential.

We know that,

dG=VdP-SdT

At constant pressure dP=0

⇒dG=-SdT

S=-dGdT∆S=-∆dGdTP

⇒dEcell°dT=∆SnF

A cell is operating reversibly if the cell potential is exactly balanced by an opposing source of potential difference.

q:
For water at 100°C and 1 bar,
Δvap H-Δvap U=¯×102 J mol-1
(Round off to the Nearest Integer)

[Assume volume of H2O(l) is much smaller than volume of H2O(g). Assume H2O(g) treated as an ideal gas]
question:
For water at 100°C and 1 bar,
Δvap H-Δvap U=¯×102 J mol-1
(Round off to the Nearest Integer)
[Use : R=8.31 J mol-1 K-1
[Assume volume of H2O(l) is much smaller than volume of H2O(g). Assume H2O(g) treated as an ideal gas]
solution:
H2O(ℓ)⇌H2O(v)
ΔH=ΔU+ΔngRT
for 1 mole waters ; Δng=1
∴ ΔngRT=1 mol×8.31 J/mol-k×373 K
=3099.63 J≅31×102 J

subject: Chemistry
q:
At 25°C, the enthalpy of the following processes are given:

H2( g)+O2( g)→2OH(g)ΔHo=78 kJ mol-1

H2( g)+1/2O2( g)→H2O(g)ΔH0=-242 kJ mol-1

H2( g)→2H(g)ΔHo=436 kJ mol-1

1/2O2( g)→O(g)ΔH0=249 kJ mol-1

What would be the value of X for the following reaction? (Nearest integer)

H2Og→Hg+OHg ∆Ho=X kJmol-1
question:
At 25°C, the enthalpy of the following processes are given:

H2( g)+O2( g)→2OH(g)ΔHo=78 kJ mol-1

H2( g)+1/2O2( g)→H2O(g)ΔH0=-242 kJ mol-1

H2( g)→2H(g)ΔHo=436 kJ mol-1

1/2O2( g)→O(g)ΔH0=249 kJ mol-1

What would be the value of X for the following reaction? (Nearest integer)

H2Og→Hg+OHg ∆Ho=X kJmol-1
solution:
2H2O(g)→2H2( g)+O2( g)  +(242×2)kJmol-1 ....(1)

H2( g)+O2( g)→2OH  +78 kJ mol-1  ....(2)

H2( g)→2H(g)  +436 kJ mol-1  ....(3)
Using Hess's law of constant heat summation, adding equations(1), (2) and (3) and dividing the net result by 2 we get:

⇒H2O(g)→H(g)+OH(g)  998×12=+499 kJ mol-1
Thus X = 499

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given :
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {sub }}^{\ominus}[\mathrm{C}(\text { graphite })]=710 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \Delta_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}=414 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \Delta_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{H}^{\Theta}=436 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \Delta_{\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}^{\Theta}=611 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
The $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus}$ for $\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}_2$ is _____ $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ (nearest integer value)
question:
Given :
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {sub }}^{\ominus}[\mathrm{C}(\text { graphite })]=710 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \Delta_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}=414 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \Delta_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{H}^{\Theta}=436 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \Delta_{\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}^{\Theta}=611 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
The $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus}$ for $\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}_2$ is _____ $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ (nearest integer value)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & {\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right]_{\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{(4)}}=2 \times\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{sub}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right]_{\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}}+2 \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}}^{\mathrm{o}}-1 \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}}^{\mathrm{o}}-4 \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{CH}}^{\mathrm{o}} } \\ \Rightarrow \quad & {\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right]_{\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}}=(2 \times 710)+(2 \times 436)-611-4 \times 414 } \\ \Rightarrow \quad & {\left[\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right]_{\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}}=25 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} }\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given

subject: Chemistry
q:
The entropy change associated with the conversion of 1 kg of ice at 273 K to water vapours at 383 K is:

(Specific heat of water liquid and water vapour are 4.2 kJ K- and 2.0 kJ K-1kg-1 ; heat of liquid fusion and vaporization of water are 334 kJ kg-1 and 2491 kJ kg-1 , respectively). ( log 273=2.436,log⁡373=2.572,log⁡383=2.583 )
question:
The entropy change associated with the conversion of 1 kg of ice at 273 K to water vapours at 383 K is:

(Specific heat of water liquid and water vapour are 4.2 kJ K- and 2.0 kJ K-1kg-1 ; heat of liquid fusion and vaporization of water are 334 kJ kg-1 and 2491 kJ kg-1 , respectively). ( log 273=2.436,log⁡373=2.572,log⁡383=2.583 )
solution:
(i ) Phase change H 2 O( l )→ H 2 O( s )

So

(ii) Temperature change phase same (fusion)

H 2 O( l ) → Δ S 2 Δ H 2 O( l )

ΔS2= mS ln T2T1=1×4.2 ln 373273=1.31

(iii) Phase change (vapourisation)

ΔS3=ΔHvap373=2491373=6.67

(iv) Temperature change phase same

H2O(g)373 K→ΔS4H2O(g)383 K

ΔS4=2 ln383373=0.0529

ΔS  total=ΔS1+ΔS2+ΔS3+ΔS4=1.223+1.31+6.67+0.0529=9.26  kJ/kg K

subject: Chemistry
q:
$20.0 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ of an ideal gas ' X ' at 600 K and 0.5 MPa undergoes isothermal reversible expansion until pressure of the gas is 0.2 MPa. Which of the following option is correct?
(Given: $\log 2=0.3010$ and $\log 5=0.6989$)
question:
$20.0 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ of an ideal gas ' X ' at 600 K and 0.5 MPa undergoes isothermal reversible expansion until pressure of the gas is 0.2 MPa. Which of the following option is correct?
(Given: $\log 2=0.3010$ and $\log 5=0.6989$)
solution:
For isothermal reversible process $\Delta U = \Delta H = 0$
$w_{iso} = -p_1 v_1 \ln \dfrac{P_1}{P_2}$
$w_{iso} = -0.5 \times 10^6 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \ln \dfrac{0.5}{0.2}$
$w_{iso} = -0.5 \times 10^6 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.303 \times (.6989 - .3010)$
$w \simeq -9.1 \text{ kJ}$
$q = -w = 9.1 \text{ kJ}$

subject: Chemistry
q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> pressure, the enthalpy of combustion of benzene <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced></math> and acetylene <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3268</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>1300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The change in enthalpy for the reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>→</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mfenced></math>, is
question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> pressure, the enthalpy of combustion of benzene <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced></math> and acetylene <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3268</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>1300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The change in enthalpy for the reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>→</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mfenced></math>, is
solution:
Standard enthalpy of combustion is defined as the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is completely burnt in oxygen with all the reactants and products in their standard state under standard conditions (298K and 1 bar pressure)

ΔHr=ΔHCreactant-ΔHCproduct

=3ΔHccC2H2,g-ΔHcC6H6,l

=3×-1300--3268

=-3900+3268=-632 kJ/mole

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the two different first order reactions given below
\(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}\) (Reaction 1\()\)
\(P \rightarrow Q\) (Reaction 2)
The ratio of the half life of Reaction 1 : Reaction 2 is \(5: 2\). If \(t_1\) and \(t_2\) represent the time taken to complete \(2 / 3^{\text {rd }}\) and \(45^{\text {th }}\) of Reaction 1 and Reaction 2, respectively, then the value of the ratio \(t_1: t_2\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1}\) (nearest integer).
[Given : \(\log _{10}(3)=0.477\) and \(\log _{10}(5)=0.699\)]
question:
Consider the two different first order reactions given below
\(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}\) (Reaction 1\()\)
\(P \rightarrow Q\) (Reaction 2)
The ratio of the half life of Reaction 1 : Reaction 2 is \(5: 2\). If \(t_1\) and \(t_2\) represent the time taken to complete \(2 / 3^{\text {rd }}\) and \(45^{\text {th }}\) of Reaction 1 and Reaction 2, respectively, then the value of the ratio \(t_1: t_2\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1}\) (nearest integer).
[Given : \(\log _{10}(3)=0.477\) and \(\log _{10}(5)=0.699\)]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_1}{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{\mathrm{II}}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_2}{\mathrm{~K}_1}=\frac{5}{2} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{K}_1 \mathrm{t}_1=\ln \frac{1}{1-\frac{2}{3}}=\ln 3 \\ & \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{t}_2=\ln \frac{1}{1-\frac{4}{5}}=\ell \mathrm{n} 5 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~K}_2} \times \frac{\mathrm{t}_1}{\mathrm{t}_2}=\frac{0.477}{0.699} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{t}_1}{\mathrm{t}_2}=\frac{0.477}{0.699} \times \frac{5}{2}=1.7=17 \times 10^{-1}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The total number of intensive properties from the following is...........
Volume, Molar heat capacity, molarity, Ecello,Gibbs free energy change, Molar mass, Mole
question:
The total number of intensive properties from the following is...........
Volume, Molar heat capacity, molarity, Ecello,Gibbs free energy change, Molar mass, Mole
solution:
An intensive property is a physical quantity whose value does not depend on the amount of substance which was measured.

Molar heat capacity, molarity, Ecello and molar mass are intensive properties.

An extensive property of a system depends on the system size or the amount of matter in the system. 

Volume, Gibbs free energy change, Mole are extensive properties.

subject: Chemistry
q: If the bond dissociation energies of $X Y, X_2$ and $Y_2$ (all diatomic molecules) are in the ratio of 1:1:0.5 and $\Delta_t \mathrm{H}$ for the formation of $X Y$ is $-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mole}^{-1}$. The bond dissociation energy of $\mathrm{X}_2$ will be
question: If the bond dissociation energies of $X Y, X_2$ and $Y_2$ (all diatomic molecules) are in the ratio of 1:1:0.5 and $\Delta_t \mathrm{H}$ for the formation of $X Y$ is $-200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mole}^{-1}$. The bond dissociation energy of $\mathrm{X}_2$ will be
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& X Y \longrightarrow X_{(g)}+Y_{(g)} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=+\mathrm{a} \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mole} \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots . .(\mathrm{i}) \\
& \mathrm{X}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{X} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=+\mathrm{a} \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mole} \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \text { (ii) } \\
& \mathrm{Y}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Y} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=+0.5 \mathrm{a} \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mole} \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots . \text { (iii) } \\
& \frac{1}{2} \times\left. \text { (ii) }+\frac{1}{2} \times \text { (iii) }-\right.\text { (i), Gives } \\
& \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{X}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Y}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{XY} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=\left(+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}+\frac{0.5}{2} \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{a}\right) \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mole} \\
& +\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}+\frac{0.5 \mathrm{a}}{2}-\mathrm{a}=-200 \\
& \mathrm{a}=800 .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: What happens when methane undergoes combustion in systems A and B respectively?
question: What happens when methane undergoes combustion in systems A and B respectively?
options:
System A System B
Temperature rises Temperature remains same

System A System B
Temperature remains same Temperature rises

System A System B
Temperature falls Temperature remains same

System A System B
Temperature falls Temperature rises
solution:
For an adiabatic free expansion of an ideal gas, the gas is kept in an insulated container and then permitted to enlarge in a vacuum. Since there is no external pressure for the gas to enlarge against, the work done by or on the system is zero. Since this process does not involve any exchange of heat transfer or work, heat will not escape and temperature of system will rise.

For diathermic container, heat will escape the container and hence temperature of container will remain same.

subject: Chemistry
q: If the enthalpy of sublimation of Li is $155 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, enthalpy of dissociation of $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ is $150 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, ionization enthalpy of Li is $520 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, electron gain enthalpy of F is $-313 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, standard enthalpy of formation of LiF is $-594 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The magnitude of lattice enthalpy of LiF is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. (Nearest Integer)
question: If the enthalpy of sublimation of Li is $155 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, enthalpy of dissociation of $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ is $150 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, ionization enthalpy of Li is $520 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, electron gain enthalpy of F is $-313 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, standard enthalpy of formation of LiF is $-594 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The magnitude of lattice enthalpy of LiF is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. (Nearest Integer)
solution:
Using Born-Haber cycle:
$\Delta H_f(LiF) = \Delta H_{sub}(Li) + \frac{1}{2}\Delta H_{diss}(F_2) + \Delta H_{ie}(Li) + \Delta H_{eg}(F) - U_{lattice}$

Substituting values: $-594 = 155 + \frac{1}{2}(150) + 520 + (-313) - U_{lattice}$

$-594 = 155 + 75 + 520 - 313 - U_{lattice}$

$-594 = 437 - U_{lattice}$

$U_{lattice} = 437 + 594 = 1031$ kJ/mol

question: The effect of temperature on spontaneity of reactions are represented as :
solution:
$\because \Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{~S}$

For spontaneity of reaction: $\Delta \mathrm{G}=-\mathrm{ve}$
